our_argument_summary: Our review says WIN-014 (China SG null) contradicts WIN-012 (positive magnetic-gravity coupling detection), arguing that a single dataset cannot simultaneously show positive detection and validate via null result. We cite Sun et al. 2010, doi:10.1016/j.pepi.2009.12.003 as the source.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: monitor.py contains ONLY one gravity-related domain: the κ coupling constant. It hardcodes KAPPA = 1.67 and computes kappa_obs = 10.9/6.5 = 1.677, then checks if error < 5%. There is NO code that monitors, validates, or tests WIN-013 or WIN-014 SG null results. These WINs exist only as static claims on the website.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: The κ value (1.67 nT/μGal) is hardcoded. The 'observed' value (10.9/6.5) is also hardcoded — no live data fetch. WIN-013 and WIN-014 have zero representation in the monitoring code.
data_sources_used: None for WIN-014 — the claim references 'Sun 2010' but no data is fetched or validated by any script
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None applicable — WIN-014 is not validated by any code at all
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Our review does not explicitly note that WIN-013/014 are completely absent from the dome's monitoring infrastructure. This is a missed opportunity — it would strengthen our argument that these are post-hoc rationalizations rather than genuine predictions.

holes_found.description: WRONG DOI: Our review cites doi:10.1016/j.pepi.2009.12.003 (attributed to 'Sun et al., 2010') for the China SG null result. This DOI actually resolves to 'Partial melt in the oceanic low velocity zone' by Hirschmann (2010) in Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors — a mantle petrology paper completely unrelated to superconducting gravimeters or eclipses. The correct reference for the 2009 China eclipse gravity observations appears to be a 2013 paper in Chinese Journal of Geophysics (doi:10.6038/cjg20130306) which used seven LaCoste-Romberg gravimeters and one SG-053 superconducting gravimeter during the July 22, 2009 total solar eclipse. Alternatively, Wikipedia's Allais effect article notes that while eight gravimeters and two pendulums were deployed across six monitoring sites in China for this eclipse, 'no result has been published in any academic journal.' If the dome model cites 'Sun 2010' but no peer-reviewed publication exists for this specific claim, that should be our critique — not a bad DOI pointing to the wrong paper.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the broken DOI immediately. Either (a) find and cite the correct paper (likely the 2013 Chinese J. Geophysics paper), or (b) note that no peer-reviewed publication of the 2009 China SG null has been identified, which would actually STRENGTHEN our critique by showing the dome model cites a non-existent or unfindable source.

holes_found.description: Imprecise 'single dataset' claim: Our review states 'A single dataset cannot simultaneously show a positive detection and validate the model through a null result.' But WIN-011/012 (Mohe 1997 + BOU 2017) and WIN-014 (China SG network 2009) are NOT the same dataset. They are different instruments at different locations in different years. The logical contradiction is real but our framing is sloppy — it's not about a single dataset, it's about the model claiming credit for opposite outcomes across different experiments.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite to clarify: 'The dome model claims positive gravity anomaly detection (WIN-011, Mohe 1997) AND null results (WIN-013/014, Membach 1999, China 2009) as simultaneous confirmations. Without specifying in advance which locations/conditions should show effects vs. nulls, any measurement outcome confirms the model — the hallmark of unfalsifiability.' This is more precise and harder to rebut.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's own monitoring code (monitor.py) does not validate WIN-013 or WIN-014 at all. These WINs have zero representation in the 39-domain audit engine. If these were genuine predictions, the monitoring system would check them. Their absence from the code reveals they are post-hoc rationalizations appended to the WIN list, not testable predictions.
sources: monitor.py Domain #7 (only gravity domain), which checks only κ = 1.67

stronger_arguments.description: The dome model's source reference ('Sun 2010') may not correspond to any published peer-reviewed paper. Wikipedia's Allais effect article states that for the July 22, 2009 China eclipse observations, 'no result has been published in any academic journal.' If the dome cites a source that doesn't exist or can't be verified, this is a citation fabrication problem — far more damaging than merely misinterpreting data.
sources: Chinese Journal of Geophysics 2013 doi:10.6038/cjg20130306 (possible actual source, published 2013 not 2010)

stronger_arguments.description: The actual scientific literature on eclipse gravity anomalies shows NO unambiguous detections with modern instruments. Yang and Van Flandern's comprehensive analysis concluded there have been 'no unambiguous detections' in recent decades with proper experimental controls. The Mohe 1997 result (WIN-011) cited by the dome is one of the few claimed positive results, and subsequent reanalysis attributed the signal to atmospheric density changes during the eclipse. This means WIN-011's positive detection is itself suspect, which undermines the entire WIN-011/012/013/014 chain.

kernel_of_truth: The dome model correctly identifies a genuine scientific debate: whether measurable gravity anomalies occur during solar eclipses. This has been an active research question since Allais (1954), and measurements during eclipses have occasionally produced suggestive but irreproducible anomalies. The model also correctly notes that superconducting gravimeters are the gold standard for such measurements, and that null results from SGs constrain the possible magnitude of any eclipse gravity effect. The model's implicit claim — that the pattern of positive vs. null results is physically meaningful — has a germ of validity, since location-dependent effects could theoretically exist. The problem is that the dome model doesn't predict WHICH locations should show effects, making the claim unfalsifiable.
deeper_analysis:
WIN-014 claims that a null result from the China superconducting gravimeter network during the July 22, 2009 total solar eclipse 'replicates WIN-013' and confirms the dome model. Our review correctly identifies this as misleading — claiming credit for both positive detections (WIN-011) and null results (WIN-013/014) makes the model unfalsifiable. However, our current rebuttal has a significant credibility problem: the DOI we cite (10.1016/j.pepi.2009.12.003) resolves to 'Partial melt in the oceanic low velocity zone' by Hirschmann (2010), a mantle petrology paper with no connection to gravimeters or eclipses. This broken citation is the kind of error that undermines reader trust in our entire review.

The dome model references 'Sun 2010' as its source, but identifying the actual paper is non-trivial. The most likely candidate is a 2013 publication in the Chinese Journal of Geophysics (doi:10.6038/cjg20130306) which reports on observations from seven LaCoste-Romberg gravimeters and one SG-053 superconducting gravimeter during the 2009 eclipse. Notably, Wikipedia's article on the Allais effect states that while instruments were deployed across six monitoring sites in China for this eclipse, 'no result has been published in any academic journal' — suggesting the dome may be citing unpublished or informally communicated results, which would be a more serious problem than our review currently highlights.

Our logical argument also needs sharpening. We currently say 'A single dataset cannot simultaneously show a positive detection and validate the model through a null result.' But WIN-011 (Mohe 1997), WIN-013 (Membach 1999), and WIN-014 (China 2009) are three different datasets from different instruments, locations, and years. The contradiction is real but our framing is imprecise. A smart defender could say: 'These are different experiments testing different aspects of the theory — the positive detection at Mohe confirms EM-gravity coupling exists, while the null results elsewhere confirm it's not universal gravitational shielding.' The proper rebuttal is: the dome model never specified IN ADVANCE which locations should show effects vs. nulls, so the claim is unfalsifiable post-hoc rationalization.

The strongest version of our argument would combine three elements: (1) the dome's own monitoring code doesn't validate WIN-013/014 at all — they exist only as static website text, not as tested predictions; (2) the cited source may not exist as a published paper, raising citation integrity questions; and (3) the broader scientific consensus, as summarized by Yang and Van Flandern, is that there have been 'no unambiguous detections' of eclipse gravity anomalies with modern instruments, meaning WIN-011's positive detection is itself suspect. If WIN-011 falls, the entire WIN-011/012/013/014 chain collapses.

The immediate fix needed is replacing the broken DOI. Either cite the 2013 Chinese J. Geophysics paper (doi:10.6038/cjg20130306) if it matches, or explicitly note that no peer-reviewed publication of the 2009 China SG null result has been identified — which would actually strengthen our critique.
